edges.description: Barrier dysfunction manifests clinically as dry skin.
Epidermal barrier dysfunction and abnormal keratinocyte differentiation produce recurrent eczematous lesions.
Compromised barrier function and immune dysregulation increase the risk of cutaneous infection.
IL-4 and IL-13 downregulate filaggrin expression, creating a vicious cycle of barrier disruption and immune activation.
IL-31 signaling contributes to neuronal itch signaling in lesional skin.
Type 2 immune skewing promotes the extrinsic/allergic AD phenotype with elevated serum IgE.
Barrier disruption and allergic immune skewing contribute to the atopic march and food-allergy comorbidity.
Allergic immune skewing in AD is associated with allergic rhinitis as part of the atopic comorbidity spectrum.
Impaired skin barrier and allergic sensitization can precede asthma in the atopic march.
TOPK-mediated STAT3 Ser727 phosphorylation amplifies IL-6 and IL-8 production, enhancing Th2 polarization and IgE responses.
TOPK-STAT3 signaling perpetuates keratinocyte and mast cell inflammatory output, sustaining chronic lesion inflammation.
TOPK-mediated STAT3 Ser727 phosphorylation drives IL-6, IL-8, and IL-33 production, amplifying chronic skin inflammation.
Persistent itch drives repetitive scratching behavior.
IL-31 and related pruritogens activate cutaneous sensory neurons, producing intense itch.
Itch severity contributes to sleep disturbance in AD.
Mechanical barrier injury increases local inflammatory activation.
Persistent pruritus and scratching-driven barrier injury contribute to lichenified flexural lesions.
Local inflammatory amplification produces erythematous rash in active AD lesions.

evidence.reference_title: Atopic dermatitis.
evidence.snippet: Atopic dermatitis is associated with increased risk of multiple comorbidities, including food allergy, asthma, allergic rhinitis, and mental health disorders.

evidence.reference_title: Differences between pediatric and adult atopic dermatitis.
evidence.snippet: Atopic diseases commonly co-occur with AD, although most do not temporally have the "atopic march."

evidence.reference_title: Atopic dermatitis.
evidence.snippet: Atopic dermatitis is associated with increased risk of multiple comorbidities, including food allergy, asthma, allergic rhinitis, and mental health disorders.

evidence.reference_title: Pathophysiology of atopic dermatitis: Clinical implications.
evidence.snippet: An impaired skin barrier may be the initial step in the development of the atopic march as well as AD, which leads to further skin inflammation and allergic sensitization.

evidence.reference_title: Atopic Dermatitis.
evidence.snippet: This chronic disorder associated with pruritus usually starts in infancy and presents with dry skin, eczematous lesions and lichenification.

evidence.reference_title: Extrinsic and intrinsic types of atopic dermatitis.
evidence.snippet: Extrinsic or allergic AD shows high total serum IgE levels and the presence of specific IgE for environmental and food allergens... While extrinsic AD is the classical type with high prevalence, the incidence of intrinsic AD is approximately 20%

nodes.description: Loss-of-function mutations in filaggrin (FLG) and other barrier proteins lead to impaired skin barrier integrity, increased transepidermal water loss, and enhanced allergen penetration. This is the primary initiating event in many patients.

evidence.reference_title: Common loss-of-function variants of the epidermal barrier protein filaggrin are a major predisposing factor for atopic dermatitis.
evidence.snippet: Filaggrin is a key protein that facilitates terminal differentiation of the epidermis and formation of the skin barrier. Here we show that two independent loss-of-function genetic variants (R510X [sic, correct designation is R501X] and 2282del4) in the gene encoding filaggrin (FLG) are very strong predisposing factors for atopic dermatitis.

evidence.reference_title: Contrasting pathogenesis of atopic dermatitis and psoriasis--part I: clinical and pathologic concepts.
evidence.snippet: They are similar in that they are complex inherited diseases involving genes that encode immune components and structural proteins that regulate differentiation of epidermal cells. Each disease is characterized by proliferation of epidermal keratinocytes and abnormal cornification or terminal differentiation in the epidermis

evidence.reference_title: Atopic Dermatitis: A Review of Diagnosis and Treatment.
evidence.snippet: a chronic, relapsing inflammatory skin disorder characterized by dry skin, localized erythematous rash, and intense pruritus

evidence.reference_title: Common loss-of-function variants of the epidermal barrier protein filaggrin are a major predisposing factor for atopic dermatitis.
evidence.snippet: two independent loss-of-function genetic variants (R510X [sic, correct designation is R501X] and 2282del4) in the gene encoding filaggrin (FLG) are very strong predisposing factors for atopic dermatitis. These variants are carried by approximately 9% of people of European origin.

evidence.reference_title: Atopic Dermatitis.
evidence.snippet: This chronic disorder associated with pruritus usually starts in infancy and presents with dry skin, eczematous lesions and lichenification.

evidence.reference_title: Differences between pediatric and adult atopic dermatitis.
evidence.snippet: Core AD features are similar between children and adults overall, including lesions affecting flexural areas, presence of atopy, and xerosis.

evidence.reference_title: Atopic dermatitis.
evidence.snippet: Atopic dermatitis is associated with increased risk of multiple comorbidities, including food allergy, asthma, allergic rhinitis, and mental health disorders.

evidence.reference_title: Atopic Dermatitis.
evidence.snippet: AD is associated with other IgE associated disorders like allergic rhinitis, asthma, and food allergies.

nodes.description: IL-31 and other pruritogens activate cutaneous sensory neurons, generating persistent itch in atopic dermatitis.

evidence.reference_title: Pathophysiology of atopic dermatitis: Clinical implications.
evidence.snippet: New insights into the pathophysiology of AD have focused on epidermal lipid profiles, neuroimmune interactions, and microbial dysbiosis.

evidence.reference_title: Atopic Dermatitis: A Review of Diagnosis and Treatment.
evidence.snippet: Atopic dermatitis, more commonly known as atopic eczema, is a chronic, relapsing inflammatory skin disorder characterized by dry skin, localized erythematous rash, and intense pruritus.

evidence.reference_title: Disseminated bullous impetigo and atopic dermatitis: Case series and literature review.
evidence.snippet: Due to compromised barrier function and immune dysregulation, children with atopic dermatitis (AD) are at increased risk of cutaneous infections

evidence.reference_title: An integrated analysis of herpes virus infections from eight randomized clinical studies of baricitinib in adults with moderate-to-severe atopic dermatitis.
evidence.snippet: Atopic dermatitis (AD) is associated with an increased risk for viral infections including those caused by herpes simplex virus and varicella zoster virus.

nodes.description: Repetitive scratching causes mechanical skin injury and worsens epidermal barrier dysfunction.
Barrier injury from scratching perpetuates inflammatory signaling and contributes to chronic lesion persistence.

evidence.reference_title: Exploring the interplay of atopic dermatitis severity with sleep and mental health: a case-control study in adult patients.
evidence.snippet: AD patients reported lower sleep quality (p = 0.002), more severe insomnia (p = 0.006) and depression (p = 0.013), and higher stress levels than healthy adults (p = 0.049).

nodes.description: Small-molecule inhibitors of TOPK (PBK) that target the TOPK-STAT3 signaling axis in keratinocytes and mast cells. Pharmacological or genetic TOPK inhibition attenuates AD-like pathological features and reduces inflammatory cytokine levels (IL-6, IL-8, IL-33, IgE).

TOPK (T-Lymphokine-Activated Killer cell-Originated Protein Kinase) is upregulated in atopic dermatitis lesions and directly phosphorylates STAT3 at Ser727 in keratinocytes and mast cells, amplifying inflammatory responses including IL-6, IL-8, and IL-33 production. TOPK-driven STAT3 activation contributes to the maintenance of AD-associated inflammation.

evidence.reference_title: Targeting the TOPK-STAT3 pathway in keratinocytes and mast cells mediates the therapeutic attenuation of Atopic dermatitis.
evidence.snippet: TOPK was found to directly interact with and phosphorylate STAT3 at Ser727. Inhibition of TOPK in both HaCaT keratinocytes and mast cells decreased STAT3 phosphorylation at Ser727, thereby alleviating AD-related inflammatory responses.

evidence.reference_title: Targeting the TOPK-STAT3 pathway in keratinocytes and mast cells mediates the therapeutic attenuation of Atopic dermatitis.
evidence.snippet: Genetic or pharmacological inhibition of TOPK markedly attenuated AD-like pathological features and reduces the levels of IgE, IL-6, IL-8 and IL-33

nodes.description: Th2-skewed immune response drives the hallmark inflammation. IL-4, IL-13, and IL-31 promote IgE production, eosinophil recruitment, and pruritus. IL-4/IL-13 signaling through JAK1/STAT6 further impairs barrier function by downregulating filaggrin and other barrier proteins.

evidence.reference_title: Pathophysiology of atopic dermatitis: Clinical implications.
evidence.snippet: Type 2 cytokines as well as interleukin 17 and interleukin 22 contribute to skin barrier dysfunction and the development of AD.

evidence.reference_title: Contrasting pathogenesis of atopic dermatitis and psoriasis--part I: clinical and pathologic concepts.
evidence.snippet: skin lesions contain immune infiltrates of T cells, dendritic cells, and other types of leukocytes. We review similarities between the diseases and differences in epidermal barrier defects and immune cells.

evidence.reference_title: Atopic Dermatitis: A Review of Diagnosis and Treatment.
evidence.snippet: Atopic dermatitis, more commonly known as atopic eczema, is a chronic, relapsing inflammatory skin disorder characterized by dry skin, localized erythematous rash, and intense pruritus.
